A Leadership Narrative Workbook
Storytelling
Workbook.
A framework for building narratives that engage,
align and inspire people to change.
Rusholme Ltd
Introduction

Welcome,

Leadership is not just about decisions, strategy, or execution. It is fundamentally about communication — and more specifically, about the power of story to move people from where they are to where they need to be.

The most effective leaders we know are those who can craft a compelling narrative: one that helps people understand the "why", feel the urgency of change, and believe in the possibility of a better future.

Great storytellers are not born — they are made. The skills required to build and tell powerful stories can be learned, practised and refined. This workbook is your guide to doing exactly that.

This programme is designed to help you develop a strategic narrative for your leadership context. You will leave with a clear, compelling story to share with your teams, stakeholders, and colleagues.

By the end of the workshop you will:

  • Understand the five-chapter narrative structure and how it applies to your leadership challenge
  • Have developed your own strategic narrative using the Storytelling framework
  • Have identified illustrative stories to bring each chapter of your narrative to life

This workbook is your personal companion. Use it to capture your thinking, refine your ideas, and build your narrative over time.

Storytelling Workbook
Introduction to the Strategic Narrative

Before we begin building your narrative, we want to introduce the key concepts that will underpin your work throughout this programme.

Take a moment to consider the questions below before we begin.

Strategic narratives are more than messages or talking points. They are coherent, emotionally resonant accounts of where we have come from, where we are, and where we are going.

The best narratives give people a sense of identity, direction, and hope — all at once.

In this workshop, you will build your own five-chapter strategic narrative using a framework that has been tested and refined with hundreds of leaders across a range of industries.

The questions below are designed to get you thinking before we begin.

1. What is a strategic narrative? And what is the difference between stories and narratives?
2. Why is it critical that leaders master these skills in the current landscape?
3. How have stories been used effectively and who are the great storytellers in your organisation?
Storytelling Workbook
Storytelling Workbook
Framing your leadership challenge

Before you start building your narrative, it is important to be clear about the leadership challenge you are trying to address.

Use the questions on the right to identify the key elements of your challenge. These will form the foundation of your five-chapter narrative.

What significant change or improvement are you aiming to achieve? Why now?
Who needs to be engaged and what role will they play?
How do your people currently feel?
How will you know if you've succeeded in engaging them?
Storytelling Workbook
Chapter One
Developing your narrative
Chapter One
Our pride and purpose…
How do we support our company's purpose?
What are our people most proud of?
What makes you personally proud to be part of this team?
What strengths do we demonstrate at our best?
What have we achieved?
Headline: we are…
Theme One  ·  e.g. purpose
Theme Two  ·  e.g. pride
Theme Three  ·  e.g. strengths
Theme Four  ·  e.g. achievements
Storytelling Workbook
Chapter Two
Developing your narrative
Chapter Two
Our current reality…
What external forces of change are shaping your organisation's future?
What challenges or barriers are holding us back?
What narratives exist in the organisation today?
What happens if we fail to change?
Headline: but…
Theme One  ·  e.g. external challenges
Theme Two  ·  e.g. internal challenges
Theme Three  ·  e.g. internal challenges
Theme Four  ·  e.g. consequences
Storytelling Workbook
Chapter Three
Developing your narrative
Chapter Three
Our fundamental opportunity…
What single ambition will make the biggest difference?
How would you describe the fundamental shift we need?
What opportunity will this unlock?
If we were to create a 'rallying cry', what would it be?
Headline: so…
Theme One  ·  e.g. ambition
Theme Two  ·  e.g. the shift we need to make
Theme Three  ·  e.g. opportunity
Theme Four  ·  e.g. rallying cry
Storytelling Workbook
Chapter Four
Developing your narrative
Chapter Four
The journey our business will take…
What investments or steps will the leadership team make?
What resources or capabilities will we need to build?
In what way can everyone contribute?
What specific changes will people need to make?
Headline: by…
Theme One  ·  e.g. commitments
Theme Two  ·  e.g. commitments
Theme Three  ·  e.g. contribution
Theme Four  ·  e.g. contribution
Storytelling Workbook
Chapter Five
Developing your narrative
Chapter Five
The desired destination…
How will we know when we have reached our destination?
What will success look and feel like?
What will the benefits of this journey be?
What stories will people tell about your organisation?
Headline: we will…
Theme One  ·  e.g. business
Theme Two  ·  e.g. customers
Theme Three  ·  e.g. people
Theme Four  ·  e.g. community
Storytelling Workbook
Developing your narrative: headline level

You have now built the raw material for your five-chapter narrative. The next step is to distil this into a headline narrative — a short, memorable version that captures the essence of each chapter.

Use the boxes below to capture a headline sentence for each chapter. These will form the spine of your strategic narrative.

Chapter One
pride and purpose
We are…
Chapter Two
current reality
But…
Chapter Three
our fundamental opportunity
So…
Chapter Four
the journey
By…
Chapter Five
desired destination
We will…
Storytelling Workbook
Bringing your
Story to Life
Begin to craft a story which illustrates the key theme of each chapter.
In the workshop, you'll work in small groups to share these and get feedback.
Storytelling Workbook
Bringing your Story to Life

You have built your five-chapter narrative. Now it is time to bring it to life with illustrative stories — real examples that make your narrative vivid, credible and memorable.

For each chapter, you will identify a story from your own experience or from your organisation that captures the essence of that chapter's theme.

Stories are the difference between a narrative that is intellectually understood and one that is truly felt.

Illustrative Stories

A well-chosen illustrative story can:

  • Make abstract ideas concrete and tangible
  • Create an emotional connection with your audience
  • Make your narrative memorable and repeatable

The best stories are specific, human, and honest. They do not need to be dramatic — they need to be true to the theme.

Stories can come from anywhere — a customer interaction, a team achievement, a moment of failure and recovery, or an example from another organisation or industry.

The key is to choose stories that resonate with your audience and that authentically illustrate the theme of each chapter.

The structure below may help you to identify the right kind of story for each chapter.

Storytelling Workbook
Bringing your Story to Life
Each chapter of your narrative is designed to evoke a specific emotion. When choosing your illustrative stories, keep these target emotions in mind — they are the emotional engine of your narrative.
Chapter One
Pride and
belonging
Chapter Two
Urgency
Chapter Three
Inspiration
Chapter Four
Belief and
clarity
Chapter Five
Hope
Storytelling Workbook
Story
Chapter One
Roots &
Identity

Andrew Carnegie arrived in Pittsburgh from Scotland at the age of 13 with nothing. He educated himself at night school. He built the world's largest steel company. And in 1900, when he was the richest man on earth, he gave $1 million back to the city — not to a hospital, not to a monument — but to a school, built for the sons and daughters of his own mill workers. He called it the Carnegie Technical Schools. Its motto: “My heart is in the work.”

In 1979, while the mills along the Monongahela were closing one by one, Carnegie Mellon University quietly created something nobody noticed — the world's first robotics programme, funded by a $3 million grant from Westinghouse.

In 1997, the last steel mill in Pittsburgh closed. Today its building — same steel frame, same riverside — houses Carnegie Mellon's robotics and AI research campus. The grandchildren of steelworkers write code where their grandparents poured iron. The motto hasn't changed.

Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania
Chapter One
Your Story
Write your own story here
Storytelling Workbook
Story
Chapter Two
Fault Lines

In August 2023, more than 200 ships were queued outside the Panama Canal. Not because of a war, or a strike, or a systems failure — because it hadn't rained. The lake that powers the canal's locks had dropped to its lowest level in recorded history.

Ships that normally waited four days were waiting three weeks. Bananas from Ecuador rotted in containers. LNG tankers rerouted around the southern tip of South America, adding ten days and thousands of tonnes of extra emissions per journey.

Five percent of all global trade flows through that canal. It was stopped — not by geopolitics, but by a drought. The fault line isn't coming. It's already running beneath us, and every season it runs a little deeper.

Panama Canal, 2023
Chapter Two
Your Story
Write your own story here
Storytelling Workbook
Story
Chapter Three
Reasons to
Believe

In 2024, scientists at CSIRO in Australia placed small mesh boxes on the floor of the Great Barrier Reef. Inside were millions of coral larvae — grown in a lab, ready to settle. The first trial, at Lizard Island, recorded settlement rates 56 times higher than any previous method. Fish species not seen in years began returning within months.

The technology exists. The knowledge exists. We mapped the human genome. We built mRNA vaccines in eleven months. We connected five billion people.

We are not a species that lacks the capability to solve hard problems. We are a species that sometimes needs to decide that a problem is ours to solve. We can. We have everything we need except that decision.

Lizard Island, Great Barrier Reef, 2024
Chapter Three
Your Story
Write your own story here
Storytelling Workbook
Story
Chapter Four
No Going Back

In September 2022, Yvon Chouinard walked into a room and told his employees that he was giving away Patagonia. Not selling it. Not floating it. Giving it — all $3 billion of it — to a trust whose sole purpose is to fight the climate crisis.

He updated the company's website that day. It now reads: “Earth is now our only shareholder.” Every dollar of profit that isn't reinvested goes directly to environmental work. Around $100 million a year.

Not a pledge. Not a target for 2050. A legal, irrevocable structure, built so that no future board, no future investor, no future pressure could undo it. That is what belief looks like when it stops being a value on a wall and becomes a decision you can't take back.

Patagonia, September 2022
Chapter Four
Your Story
Write your own story here
Storytelling Workbook
Story
Chapter Five
Imagine When

Imagine a morning when the CEO of a shipping company checks the Panama Canal and the water levels are stable — and have been for twenty years. When a procurement director reviews her supply chain and every tier is measured, transparent, and improving.

When a scientist at Lizard Island dives on a reef she helped restore and counts species she only ever saw in textbooks. When the energy powering this room comes from somewhere clean, costs less than it used to, and employs more people than what came before.

That world is not fantasy — every single part of it is already happening somewhere, for someone, right now. What we are deciding, in rooms like this one, is how much of it becomes everywhere.

Chapter Five
Your Story
Write your own story here

Storytelling Workbook
Additional Resources
Why storytelling?

Storytelling is one of humanity's oldest and most powerful communication tools. Long before writing was invented, stories were the primary way knowledge, values and culture were transmitted from one generation to the next.

In the modern organisation, storytelling is equally powerful. Research shows that facts wrapped in a story are up to 22 times more memorable than facts presented alone.

Volatility
Vision
Uncertainty
Understanding & Unity
Complexity
Clarity & Connection
Ambiguity
Agility with an Anchor
A structure for your Illustrative Story
·  A great example of… (choose a theme from one of the key messages)
·  … is this story about… (hero or heroes)
·  … when faced with… (situation)
·  … they knew that… (what they were thinking)
·  … so, they responded by… (action / choice different from the norm)
·  … and in doing so… (consequence or outcome)
·  … imagine if… (extrapolation — team, organisation, society as a whole)

About the facilitators
Sara Curleigh-Parsons
Facilitator · Executive Coach · Consultant · Storyteller
EMCC Accredited Coach · 25+ years experience
Sara is a leadership facilitator, executive coach and storyteller with over 25 years of experience helping leaders find their voice, shape their narrative and bring people with them.
She works with organisations including The Storytellers (now part of Accenture), London Business School and HEC Paris. She is co-author of Uncover Your Light: Stories of Hope and Resilience.
sara.parsons@rusholme.org  ·  +44 7903 682140
Juliet Fallowfield
Executive Coach · Facilitator · Strategic Writer · Storyteller
ICF Accredited Coach · 30+ years experience
Juliet is a leadership and systems coach, facilitator and strategic writer with over 30 years of international experience.
Her approach draws on systems thinking, narrative therapy and ontological coaching, developed through work across Canada, North and South-east Asia, France, Germany and the UK.
juliet@nowtonext.co.uk  ·  +44 7432 652 940

Acknowledgements

Storytelling Workbook was developed by Sara Parsons and Juliet Fallowfield of Now to Next, drawing on decades of experience working with leaders across a wide range of organisations and industries.

We are grateful to all the leaders, teams and organisations who have contributed their stories, their insights and their trust to the development of this programme. Your experiences are the heart of this work.
